title: Apply: electric and magnetic forces
description: Apply your knowledge of electric and magnetic forces in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students has two balloons, labeled A and B. They rub the balloons with a blanket to give them negative $(\bold{-})$ electric charge. Then, the students hold the balloons a distance apart, as modeled below. The balloons exert electric forces on each other.

 [[☃ image 5]]

Next, the student holding balloon A stays in place while the student holding balloon B moves backward, as modeled below.

 [[☃ image 6]]

**As B is moved away, what happens to the strengths of the electric forces?**

* The electric force A exerts on B [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The electric force B exerts on A [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
The electric forces two objects exert on each other depends on the the amount of charge they have, and the distance between them.

As balloon B is moved away, the amount of charge on the balloons doesn't change, but the distance between them does.
The farther apart two charged objects are, the weaker the electric forces are. So, as balloon B is moved away the electric forces get weaker.

The two balloons are interacting with each other. According to Newton's third law, the electric forces they exert on each other at any time must have *equal strengths*. 

So, the electric forces on both balloons get weaker by the same amount. It doesn't matter that one moved and the other stayed in place.

The answer is:

* The electric force A exerts on B gets weaker.

* The electric force B exerts on A gets weaker.

options.alt: Diagram showing two negatively charged balloons held a distance apart. Balloon A is on the left and balloon B is on the right.
Balloon A is still in its original position. Balloon B has been moved farther to the right so the balloons are farther apart.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students has two balloons, labeled A and B. They rub the balloons with a blanket to give them negative $(\bold{-})$ electric charge. Then, the students hold the balloons a distance apart, as modeled below. The balloons exert electric forces on each other.

 [[☃ image 7]]

Next, the student holding balloon A moves forward while the student holding balloon B stays in place, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 8]]

**As A is moved closer, what happens to the strengths of the electric forces?**

* The electric force A exerts on B [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The electric force B exerts on A [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
The electric forces two objects exert on each other depends on the the amount of charge they have, and the distance between them.

As balloon A is moved closer, the amount of charge on the balloons doesn't change, but the distance between them does.
The closer together two charged objects are, the stronger the electric forces are. So, as balloon A is moved closer the electric forces get stronger.

The two balloons are interacting with each other. According to Newton's third law, the electric forces they exert on each other at any time must have *equal strengths*. 

So, the electric forces on both balloons get stronger by the same amount. It doesn't matter that one moved and the other stayed in place.

The answer is:

* The electric force A exerts on B gets stronger.

* The electric force B exerts on A gets stronger.

options.alt: Diagram showing two negatively charged balloons held a distance apart. Balloon A is on the left and balloon B is on the right.
Balloon A has been moved farther to the right, so the balloons are closer. Balloon B is still in its original position.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students has two balloons, labeled A and B. They rub the balloons with a blanket to give them negative $(\bold{-})$ electric charge. Then, the students hold the balloons a distance apart, as modeled below. The balloons exert electric forces on each other.

 [[☃ image 7]]

Next, balloon A is rubbed with the blanket again to give it more charge. Both students stay in their original positions, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 9]]

**When the amount of charge on A increases, what happens to the strengths of the electric forces?**

* The electric force A exerts on B [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The electric force B exerts on A [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
The electric forces two objects exert on each other depends on the the amount of charge they have, and the distance between them.

The amount of charge on balloon A changes. The distance between the balloons doesn't change.
The more charge one or both objects have, the stronger the electric forces are. So, when the amount of charge on balloon A increases, the electric forces get stronger.

The two balloons are interacting with each other. According to Newton's third law, the electric forces they exert on each other at any time must have *equal strengths*. 

So, the electric forces on both balloons get stronger by the same amount. It doesn't matter that the amount of charge increased on one of the balloons and stayed the same on the other.

The answer is:

* The electric force A exerts on B gets stronger.

* The electric force B exerts on A gets stronger.

options.alt: Diagram showing two negatively charged balloons held a distance apart. Balloon A is on the left and balloon B is on the right.
The amount of negative charge on balloon A has increased. The amount of charge on balloon B hasn't changed. Both balloons are in their original positions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A girl slides down a plastic playground slide. At the bottom, her hair stands out as shown below.

[[☃ image 7]]

**Complete the explanation of this phenomenon.**

* The girl's hairs are [[☃ dropdown 1]] each other.

* So, while sliding against the plastic, [[☃ dropdown 2]] charges must have built up on her hairs.
hints: The girl's hairs are pushing away from each other. This means they are repelling each other.
Electric forces follow the rule that *like charges repel and opposite charges attract*.

Since the hairs are repelling, we know like charges must have built up on them. Either the hairs all became positively charged, or all became negatively charged. (We can't tell which without more information).

The answer is:

* The girl's hairs are repelling each other.

* So, while sliding against the plastic, like charges must have built up on her hairs.

options.alt: A girl slides down a plastic playground slide. When she reaches the bottom her hairs are standing up and away from each other.

assessment_items.question_content:
A **Van de Graaff generator** builds up electric charge on a metal dome. If metal pie pans are stacked on top and the generator is turned on, the pans fly off as shown below.

[[☃ image 4]]

**Complete the explanation of this phenomenon.**

* The pie pans are [[☃ dropdown 1]] each other.

* So, when the generator is turned on, [[☃ dropdown 2]] charges must build up on the pans.
hints: The pie pans are pushing away from each other. This means they are repelling each other.
Electric forces follow the rule that *like charges repel and opposite charges attract*.

Since the pans are repelling, we know like charges must have built up on them. Either the pans all became positively charged, or all became negatively charged. (We can't tell which without more information).

The answer is:

* The pie pans are repelling each other.

* So, when the generator is turned on, like charges must build up on the pans.

options.alt: A large metal dome with a stack of lightweight metal pie pans on top. The pans fly off the dome, one at a time.

assessment_items.question_content:
A cat rubs against a balloon. Afterward, the balloon sticks to the cat as shown below.

  [[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the explanation of this phenomenon.**

* The cat and balloon are [[☃ dropdown 1]] each other.

* So, when the cat rubbed against the balloon, [[☃ dropdown 2]] charges must have built up on the cat and balloon.
hints: The balloon is sticking to the cat. This means they are attracting each other.
Electric forces follow the rule that *like charges repel and opposite charges attract*.

Since the cat and balloon are attracting, we know opposite charges must have built up on them. Either the cat became positively charged and the balloon became negatively charged, or vice versa. (We can't tell which without more information).

The answer is:

* The cat and balloon are attracting each other.

* So, when the cat rubbed against the balloon, opposite charges must have built up on the cat and balloon.

options.alt: A cat moves around with a balloon stuck to his hair.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student uses a magnetic field sensor to measure the  field of a permanent magnet. They use a ruler to measure the distance from the magnet to the sensor, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

The student moves the sensor and records the magnetic field strength in [[☃ definition 1]] $(\text{mT})$ at different distances in centimeters $(\text{cm}).$ A graph of their data is shown below.

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]

**Analyze the data to complete the statements.**

* As distance from the magnet increases, the magnetic field strength [[☃ dropdown 4]]. 

* When the distance from the magnet doubles from $3\,\text{cm}$ to $6\,\text{cm},$ the magnetic field becomes [[☃ dropdown 3]] as strong.
hints:
The pattern on the graph shows that points with larger distance values (are farther right on the graph) have smaller magnetic field strength values (are farther down on the  graph).

This means that as distance from the magnet increases, the magnetic field strength decreases.

We can analyze the numbers to be more specific about how the field strength changes with distance. Let’s see how the field strength changes when distance from the magnet doubles from $3\,\text{cm}$ to $6\,\text{cm}.$

 [[☃ image 1]]

At a distance of $\color{#0000ff}3\,\text{cm}\color{#000000},$ the magnetic field strength is $\color{#0000ff}2\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$

At a distance of $\color{#ff0000}6\,\text{cm}\color{#000000},$ the magnetic field strength is approximately $\color{#ff0000}0.25\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$

$\color{#ff0000}0.25\,\text{mT}$ is *less than half* of $\color{#0000ff}2\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$

So, when the distance doubles, the magnetic field drops to less than half of its original strength.

The answer is:

* As distance from the magnet increases, the magnetic field strength decreases.

* When the distance from the magnet doubles from $3\,\text{cm}$ to $6\,\text{cm},$ the magnetic field becomes less than half as strong.

options.definition: The tesla $(\text{T})$ is a unit of magnetic field strength. One tesla equals one thousand milliteslas $(\text{mT}).$

options.alt: A small magnet is placed at the 0 centimeter mark of a ruler. A hand holds a magnetic field sensor. The tip of the sensor is located at the 3 centimeter mark of the ruler.

options.fullGraphAriaDescription: Graph with magnetic field strength in milliteslas on the vertical axis, and distance in centimeters on the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student uses a magnetic field sensor to measure the  field of a permanent magnet. They use a ruler to measure the distance from the magnet to the sensor, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The student moves the sensor and records the magnetic field strength in [[☃ definition 1]] $(\text{mT})$ at different distances in centimeters $(\text{cm}).$ A graph of their data is shown below.

[[☃ interactive-graph 2]]

**Analyze the data to complete the statements.**

* As distance from the magnet decreases, the magnetic field strength [[☃ dropdown 4]]. 

* When the distance from the magnet is cut in half from $6\,\text{cm}$ to $3\,\text{cm},$ the magnetic field becomes [[☃ dropdown 3]] as strong.
hints:
The pattern on the graph shows that points with smaller distance values (are farther left on the graph) have larger magnetic field strength values (are farther up on the  graph).

This means that as distance to the magnet decreases, the magnetic field strength increases.

We can analyze the numbers to be more specific about how the field strength changes. Let’s see how the field strength changes when distance from the magnet is cut in half from $6\,\text{cm}$ to $3\,\text{cm}.$

 [[☃ image 1]]

At a distance of $\color{#ff0000}6\,\text{cm}\color{#000000},$ the magnetic field strength is approximately $\color{#ff0000}0.25\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$

At a distance of $\color{#0000ff}3\,\text{cm}\color{#000000},$ the magnetic field strength is $\color{#0000ff}2\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$

$\color{#0000ff}2\,\text{mT}$ is *more than two times* $\color{#ff0000}0.25\,\text{mT}\color{#000000}.$ 

So, when the distance is cut in half, the magnetic field grows to more than two times its original strength.

The answer is:

* As distance from the magnet decreases, the magnetic field strength increases.

* When the distance from the magnet is cut in half from $6\,\text{cm}$ to $3\,\text{cm},$ the magnetic field becomes more than two times as strong.

options.definition: The tesla $(\text{T})$ is a unit of magnetic field strength. One tesla equals one thousand milliteslas $(\text{mT}).$

options.alt: A small magnet is placed at the 0 centimeter mark of a ruler. A hand holds a magnetic field sensor. The tip of the sensor is located at the 3 centimeter mark of the ruler.

options.fullGraphAriaDescription: Graph with magnetic field strength in milliteslas on the vertical axis, and distance in centimeters on the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student uses a magnetic field sensor to measure the  field of a permanent magnet. They use a ruler to measure the distance from the magnet to the sensor, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The student moves the sensor and records the magnetic field strength in [[☃ definition 1]] $(\text{mT})$ at different distances in centimeters $(\text{cm}).$ A graph of their data is shown below.

[[☃ interactive-graph 2]]

**Analyze the graph to complete the prediction.**

* The student replaces the original magnet with a weaker magnet of the same shape and size. The weaker magnet will have a field strength of $2\,\text{mT}$ at a distance of [[☃ dropdown 1]] $3\,\text{cm}.$
hints:
The pattern on the graph shows that points with larger distance values (are farther right on the graph) have smaller magnetic field strength values (are farther down on the  graph).

This means that as distance from the magnet increases, the magnetic field strength decreases.

The graph tells us the original magnet has a field strength of $2\,\text{mT}$ at a distance of exactly $3\,\text{cm}.$

 [[☃ image 2]]
We're told the student replaces the original magnet with a *weaker* magnet. So, to experience the same $2\,\text{mT}$ field strength, we'd need to be closer to it compared to he stronger magnet.

The answer is:

* The student replaces the original magnet with a weaker magnet of the same shape and size. The weaker magnet will have a field strength of $2\,\text{mT}$ at a distance of less than $3\,\text{cm}.$

options.definition: The tesla $(\text{T})$ is a unit of magnetic field strength. One tesla equals one thousand milliteslas $(\text{mT}).$

options.alt: A small magnet is placed at the 0 centimeter mark of a ruler. A hand holds a magnetic field sensor. The tip of the sensor is located at the 3 centimeter mark of the ruler.

options.fullGraphAriaDescription: Graph with magnetic field strength in milliteslas on the vertical axis, and distance in centimeters on the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students builds an **electromagnet** by turning wire around a nail, and then connecting the ends of the wire to a battery. The students can hang some number of paper clips from the nail, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The students predict that increasing the number of turns of wire around the nail will increase the strength of the electromagnet. They plan an investigation to test their prediction.

**What should they select as the [[☃ definition 1]] for their investigation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: The **independent variable** is what's changed on purpose, to observe its effect on another variable.

options.alt: Diagram shows a nail held vertically above a table. A wire is wrapped multiple times around the nail. The ends of the wire are connected to a battery. Two paperclips hang from the end of the nail

choices.content: number of turns of wire around the nail
number of paperclips that hang from the nail

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students builds an **electromagnet** by turning wire around a nail, and then connecting the ends of the wire to a battery. The students can hang some number of paper clips from the nail, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The students predict that increasing the number of turns of wire around the nail will increase the strength of the electromagnet. They plan an investigation to test their prediction.

**What should they select as the [[☃ definition 1]] for their investigation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: The **dependent variable** is what's measured as the outcome, to see if it was affected by another variable.

options.alt: Diagram shows a nail held vertically above a table. A wire is wrapped multiple times around the nail. The ends of the wire are connected to a battery. Two paperclips hang from the end of the nail

choices.content: number of turns of wire around the nail
number of paperclips that hang from the nail

[[☃ radio 1]]
